coredata.dc:title: Research on the spectral properties of luminescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Li-ping
Wang, Xin-Xing
Lin, Shao-Qin
Zhang, Li-Hong
Lin, Chang-Qing
Li, Zhi-Ming
Liu, Jia-Ming
coredata.dc:description:
This paper is trying to research the developing status of carbon dots (CDs), and the results show that the simple, rapid and high yield synthetic methods for CDs and the application of CDs in biological science and analysis field will certainly become an inevitable development trend in the future. The CDs obtained by microwave possess excellent optical properties including UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence. Under the conditions of 30°C and 10min, the fluorescence signal (F) of CDs not only could be enhanced by hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), Triton X-100, Na2S, Na2C2O4 and NH3.H2O, but also could be quenched by sodium dodecyl sulfate, KBrO3, K2S2O8, NaIO4, ascorbic acid, NaBH4, HNO3, HCl, H2SO4, CH3COOH and most metal ions, with the
λ
em
max
blue or red shifting in varying degrees, indicating the potential values of CDs in analytical application. Besides, the sensitive response of F to pH showed the promise of developing a new pH sensor with CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Synthesis
Characterization
Spectral properties
Analytical application

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phosphorescence spectra, contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra
Fluorescence spectra, contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra
Factors influencing the fluorescence characteristics of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phosphorescence spectra of CDs(1.1’ NCM, 2.2’ 1.1’+1.00mL CDs).
Contour map and three-dimensional phosphorescence spectra of CDs.
Contour map and three-dimensional phosphorescence spectra of NCM.
Fluorescence spectra, contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs (1.00ml CDs solution).

Different ions influencing on fluorescence characteristics of CDs (a: CDs; b: As5+; c: Ti4+; d: Hg2+; e: Se4+; f: Mo5+; g: Pd2+; h: Zn2+; i: Be4+; j: Mg2+; k: Cd2+; l: Sr4+; m: Co2+; n: Cu2+; o: Cr3+; p: Zr4+; q: Mn5+; r: Sb4+; s: Ag+; t: Pb2+; u: Fe3+).
Effects of oxidants and reducers on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs (a: CDs; b: KBrO3; c: KIO4; d: H2O2; e: ascorbic acid; f: Na2S; g: NaBH4; h: Na2C2O4).
Effects of alkali and acid on the F of CDs (a: CDs; b: H2SO4; c: HCl; d: HNO3; e: CH3COOH; f: NaOH; g: NH3.H2O).
Relative standard deviation of CDs and each ion (n =6).

head.ce:title: Research on the spectral properties of luminescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Zhangzhou Normal College, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Fujian Education College, Fuzhou 350001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Biological Engineering, Zhangzhou Institute of Technology, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► The current research, development trend and focus of carbon dots have been summarized. ► The CDs were synthesized by microwave and characterized by phosphorescence. ► The spectral properties of CDs and their potential application have been studied.

This paper is trying to research the developing status of carbon dots (CDs), and the results show that the simple, rapid and high yield synthetic methods for CDs and the application of CDs in biological science and analysis field will certainly become an inevitable development trend in the future. The CDs obtained by microwave possess excellent optical properties including UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence. Under the conditions of 30°C and 10min, the fluorescence signal (F) of CDs not only could be enhanced by hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), Triton X-100, Na2S, Na2C2O4 and NH3.H2O, but also could be quenched by sodium dodecyl sulfate, KBrO3, K2S2O8, NaIO4, ascorbic acid, NaBH4, HNO3, HCl, H2SO4, CH3COOH and most metal ions, with the
λ
em
max
blue or red shifting in varying degrees, indicating the potential values of CDs in analytical application. Besides, the sensitive response of F to pH showed the promise of developing a new pH sensor with CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence semiconductor quantum dots (InAs, CdS and CdSe) have been increasingly applied in modern biotechnology (biological labeling [1], bio-imaging and bio-sensing [2–5]) due to their particular optical properties. However, there are also some defects, such as the harsh synthetic conditions, high cost, intrinsic cytotoxicity [6] and environmental hazards [7,8] and so on, limiting their widespread application in human. To overcome the defects, a wide variety of carbon-based nanomaterials have been synthesized including carbon nanotubes [9,10], fullerenes [11,12], nanodiamond [13,14], graphene and graphene oxide [15], and they all show potential application in many spheres as high performance materials. Especially, the recent emergence of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) has presented exciting opportunities applying in biological labeling and life science [16–18] owing to their advantages of excellent optical properties, simple synthesis, abundant sources, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Additionally, CDs have been applied as photocatalyst [19], optical sensor for Hg2+ [20,21] and on–off fluorescence probe of europium-adjusted CDs for phosphate detection [22] and so forth. These achievements not only raise the curtain on the application of CDs in biological science and analytical chemistry, and become one of the current hottest research areas, but also promote the development of the methods for their synthesis.

With the progress of application of CDs, their synthesis and characterization are greatly developing. Since CDs can be used as light emitters firstly proposed by Sun et al. in 2006 [13], diverse synthetic methods have been established, such as exfoliation from graphite [14], arc-discharge chemical oxidation of single-walled carbon nanotubes [23,24], laser ablation of carbon nanaomaterials [25], multi-walled carbon nanotubes [26] or electrochemical oxidation of graphite [27,28], thermal oxidation of glycerol [29] and l-ascorbic acid [30], dehydration of sucrose or starch using concentrated sulfuric acid [31], oxidation of candle soot [32,33] and natural gas soot [34], and microwave synthesis of glucose [35] and so on. Unfortunately, the present research mainly concentrates on the synthesis of CDs, and there is rare attention on the factors influencing the fluorescence properties (fluorescence signal and wavelength) of CDs. Therefore, we synthesized CDs with excellent optical properties by microwave and examined the different influencing factors including surfactants, oxidants, reducers, ions, luminescence molecules, pH, temperature and time on the fluorescence properties of 1.00ml CDs. Besides, the potential analytical application of CDs was discussed based on the experimental results, which not only accelerates the development of their synthesis and application, but also provides information reference for the further analytical application of CDs.

UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary Eclipse), microwave oven (Galanz) and an AE240 electronic analytical balance (Mettler–Toledo Instruments Shanghai Company) were used. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-1230 electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan) at 300kV. The acidity of all the systems was measured by the pHS-3B precision acidometer. All reagents are of analytical grade. Nanopure deionized water (18.2MΩ) was used for all experiments.

According to Ref. [35], 2.00g glucose and 10.00ml PEG-200 were mixed and dissolved in 3.00ml water to form a clear solution. Then the solution was put into a 540W microwave oven for 2min, and the yellow CDs solution was obtained. Finally, the CDs were purified and diluted to 100.00ml with water.

After being immersed in Pb(CH3COO)2 solution for 10s, the nitrocellulose membrane (NCM, diameter is 1.50cm.) was taken out and dried at 90°C for 2min. Then, 0.40μL CDs solution was added onto the indentation (diameter is 0.4cm) of NCM with a flattened head syringe (±0.01μL), and the NCM was dried at 90°C for 2min. Finally, the phosphorescence spectra of CDs and the blank solution were scanned.

Into a 10ml-comparison tube, 1.00ml CDs solution was added. Then, 1.00ml of 10.0μg different ions, 3% (w/v) acid and alkali, 0.1% (v/v) surfactants, 3% (w/v) oxidants, 3% (w/v) reducers and 1.0×10–6 moll−1 luminescence molecules were separately added, diluted to 10ml with water and mixed homogeneously. The mixture was kept at 30°C for 10min, and cooled by flowing water for 5min. Meanwhile, a reagent blank test was conducted. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of the test solution (F 1) and the blank solution (F 0) were recorded. And ΔF (=F 1 − F 0) was calculated.
The obtained CDs were characterized with TEM (Fig. 1 ), and the TEM image indicates that CDs were well distributed with the particle size of ca. 5nm, providing a basis for the following study.

As demonstrated in Fig. 2 , the as-obtained CDs show an obvious characteristic absorption centered at 278nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and tails out beyond 500nm, which is analogous to the behaviors of the semiconductor quantum dots [34]. However, the application of CDs is limited due to no characteristic absorption in the visible absorption region. To resolve this problem, scientists try to synthesize CDs with further modification [36]. Taking the work of Peng group for example, the CDs passivated with 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine owned an absorption peak in the range from 350 to 550nm [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphorescence spectra, contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para:
With the perturbation of Pb2+, CDs could emit strong and stable room temperature phosphorescence on NCM (Fig. 3
). The distinct differences between contour map and three-dimensional phosphorescence spectra lay the foundation of the application of CDs in room temperature phosphorescence field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra, contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para:
CDs could emit strong and stable fluorescence at 345.0/438.0nm (Fig. 4 ,
λ
ex
max
/
λ
em
max
, F =96.5, Excitation slit 5nm, Emission slit 5nm) well in agreement with that of Ref. [35]. And the organic and polymeric passivation agents contain no visible or near-UV chromophores and therefore obviously nonemissive in the UV/Vis range [13,35,37]. Thus, the observed bright and colorful luminescence emission is due to the surface-passivated CDs. So far, there have been several mechanisms proposed to explain the unique optical characteristics of CDs, such as the size distribution of CDs in the sample [13], different emissive trap sites [13,32,34] and the pyrolytic formation of several different polyaromatic fluorophores within the carbogenic core [38]. In this work, it has been hypothesized that the unique optical characteristics are attributed to not only the particle size but also to a distribution of different emissive sites on each passivated carbon dots which is similar to that observed in the photoluminescence of carbon nanotubes [39]. And further investigation is necessary and will be pursued. Additionally, there are remarkable differences between contour map and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra, which could be used to identify CDs and expand the analytical application of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors influencing the fluorescence characteristics of CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
In order to improve the fluorescent signal and analysis value of CDs, the influences of nonionic, cationic and anionic surfactants on the fluorescent characteristics of CDs were examined. Results show that sodium dodecyl sulfate could quench the F of CDs and was considered unsuitable as a sensitizer for CDs; CTAB and TritonX-100 caused the F of CDs sharply enhancing, and the corresponding ΔF were 260.1 and 406.3. Thereinto, TritonX-100 made the
λ
em
max
of CDs to blue shift for 135.9nm, which resulted from the formation of the micellar compound by the reaction between TritonX-100 and CDs. Obviously, on account of the enhanced effect of CTAB and TritonX-100, they can be used as sensitizers for the luminescent system of CDs. Otherwise, the –COOH group of CDs can react with the –NH2 of antibody or lectin [40,41] to form the labeling product under suitable conditions, which expand the application of CDs in biological analysis.

Common oxidants, like KBrO3, H2O2 and KIO4, could quench the F of CDs in the order of H2O2 <KBrO3 <KIO4 (Fig. 5 ). And KIO4 caused the
λ
em
max
of CDs to red shift for 10.9nm, while KBrO3 and H2O2 had no effect on the
λ
em
max
of CDs. As mentioned above, CDs could be oxidized by KBrO3, H2O2 and KIO4 to different extents indicating the reducibility of CDs, which can serve as important references for selecting an oxidant when using CDs as inductor in catalytic kinetic systems.

The effects of reducers on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs were investigated. Results indicate that 1.00ml of 3% Na2S and Na2C2O4 could enhance the F of CDs in the order of Na2S>Na2C2O4 with the
λ
em
max
of CDs red shifting for 27.9nm for Na2S but scarcely changing for Na2C2O4. One ml of 3% ascorbic acid and NaBH4 could quench the F of CDs in the order of ascorbic acid>NaBH4 with the
λ
em
max
of CDs blue shifting for 13.0nm for NaBH4 but no change for ascorbic acid (Fig. 5). Though the mechanism of Na2S and Na2C2O4 enhancing the F of CDs is not clear, the experimental results still provide a new way for enhancing the F of luminescent systems and show their application value in luminescence analysis.

The effects of different ions on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs were investigated (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6 and Table 1 , most ions caused the F of CDs with the constant
λ
em
max
and peak shape, and the highest quenching degree was caused by Fe3+ possibly because CDs were oxidized to non-fluorescence material. Similar to some ions with catalytic ability (As5+ [42], Hg2+ [43] and Cu2+ [44]), Fe3+ could catalyze KBrO3 oxidizing CDs to quench the F of the system. Besides, the concentration of Fe3+ was linearly correlated with ΔF in the range of 2.5-90.0ngml−1 with the regression equation being ΔF =72.54+0.3577mFe3+ (ngml−1) and correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9911. Thus, a new catalytic–fluorometric determination of trace Fe3+ could be developed.

The effects of luminescent molecules (1.0μmoll−1) on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs were investigated. Results demonstrate that CDs could quench the F of HFin, FITC, HFinI4Cl4 and HFinBr4 in the order of HFin<FITC<HFinI4Cl4 <HFinBr4 (not shown here) possibly due to the increased attracting electron effect of the –COOH, –I4Cl4 and –Br4 groups connecting with HFin. Thus, the determination of the content of relevant materials is of analysis value based on the quenching effect of CDs on the F of fluorescein systems.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the variation of the fluorescence characteristics of CDs was observed when strong acid and/or base was added. Thereinto, the
λ
em
max
of CDs red shifted for alkali with the greater enhancing effect of NH3.H2O than NaOH on the F of CDs, while the
λ
em
max
of CDs blue shifted for acid with the F of CDs quenching in the order of HNO3 >HCl>H2SO4 >CH3COOH. These facts can be important evidence for selecting the optimal pH in the systems.

Based on the effects of acid and alkali on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs mentioned above, we adjusted the pH of the solution with phosphate buffer solutions and found that the F of CDs enhanced with the increasing of pH of the system. When the pH value of the system was 7.0, the F of CDs reached the maximum and decreased when the pH value was over 7.0. Besides, the pH value of the system had linear relation with the F of CDs in the range of 2.0–7.0, and the linear regression equation was F =106.0+27.76 pH with the correlation coefficient (R) being 0.9913. This pH-sensitive property could provide a basis for pH sensing even though there is no exact mechanism for this interesting phenomenon [32].

Apart from the factors mentioned above, the effects of temperature and time on fluorescence characteristics of CDs were investigated. The
λ
em
max
of CDs (443.9nm) did not shift with varying temperature from 18 to 60°C and the F of CDs enhanced with the temperature increasing from 18 to 30°C but decreased when the temperature was over 30°C. The possible reason is that the higher temperature enhances the probability of CDs from ground state to excited state, correspondingly, the probability of CDs back to the ground state in the form of fluorescence emission increases, resulting in the F enhancement of CDs; while the higher the temperature is, the lower the viscosity of the solution is and the more collision possibility between CDs and other molecules is. The energy of excited CDs was transferred by means of collisions and intramolecular energy transfer, thus the excited CDs come back to the ground state in the form of non-fluorescence resulting in the F quenching of CDs. Additionally, though the
λ
em
max
of CDs (446.4nm) stayed stable with varying heating time, the F of CDs enhanced within 5–10min but decreased when the heating time was more than 10min. These results provide an optimum condition for the establishment and application of fluorometry.

In summary, the synthetic methods for CDs are developing toward the simple, rapid and high yield directions, and TEM has been the main characterization of CDs. Besides TEM, X-ray diffraction method and infrared spectrophotometry, UV–Vis spectra, fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra as well as their contour map and three-dimensional spectra can be used for qualitative analysis and fingerprint identification. The tentative exploration of different effects on the optical properties of CDs and the possibility of analytical application mentioned above reveal the potential analysis value and foreground of CDs, further promoting the research development of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Fujian Province Natural Science Foundation (Nos. 2010J01053), the Fujian Province Education Committee (JK2010035, JA10203, JA10277, JA11311 and JA11164) and the Scientific Research Program of Zhangzhou Institute of Technology Foundation (Nos. ZZY1101 and ZZY1106).
